blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلْفَجْرِ (١)
By the Dawn, (1)
وَلَيَالٍ عَشْرٍ (٢)
by the ten nights, (2)
وَٱلشَّفْعِ وَٱلْوَتْرِ (٣)
by the Even and the Odd, (3)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَسْرِ (٤)
by the night when it departs! (4)
هَلْ فِى ذَٰلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِّذِى حِجْرٍ (٥)
Is there an oath in that for one possessing intellect? (5)
أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ (٦)
Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with [the people of] ‘Ad, (6)
إِرَمَ ذَاتِ ٱلْعِمَادِ (٧)
[and] Iram, [the city] of the pillars, (7)
ٱلَّتِى لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (٨)
the like of which was not created among cities, (8)
وَثَمُودَ ٱلَّذِينَ جَابُوا۟ ٱلصَّخْرَ بِٱلْوَادِ (٩)
and [the people of] Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley, (9)
وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِى ٱلْأَوْتَادِ (١٠)
and Pharaoh, the impaler (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ طَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ (١١)
—those who rebelled [against Allah] in their cities (11)
فَأَكْثَرُوا۟ فِيهَا ٱلْفَسَادَ (١٢)
and caused much corruption in them, (12)
فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ (١٣)
so your Lord poured on them lashes of punishment. (13)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِٱلْمِرْصَادِ (١٤)
Indeed your Lord is in ambush. (14)
فَأَمَّا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَأَكْرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَكْرَمَنِ (١٥)
As for man, whenever his Lord tests him, and grants him honour, and blesses him, he says, ‘My Lord has honoured me.’ (15)
وَأَمَّآ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَهَـٰنَنِ (١٦)
But when He tests him and tightens for him his provision, he says, ‘My Lord has humiliated me.’ (16)
كَلَّا ۖ بَل لَّا تُكْرِمُونَ ٱلْيَتِيمَ (١٧)
No indeed! No, you do not honour the orphan, (17)
وَلَا تَحَـٰٓضُّونَ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ (١٨)
and do not urge the feeding of the needy. (18)
وَتَأْكُلُونَ ٱلتُّرَاثَ أَكْلًا لَّمًّا (١٩)
You eat the inheritance rapaciously, (19)
وَتُحِبُّونَ ٱلْمَالَ حُبًّا جَمًّا (٢٠)
and love wealth with much fondness. (20)
كَلَّآ إِذَا دُكَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا (٢١)
No indeed! When the earth is levelled to a plain, (21)
وَجَآءَ رَبُّكَ وَٱلْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا (٢٢)
and your Lord [’s edict] arrives with the angels in ranks, (22)
وَجِا۟ىٓءَ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِجَهَنَّمَ ۚ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ (٢٣)
the day when hell is brought [near], man will take admonition on that day, but what will that admonition avail him? (23)
يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى قَدَّمْتُ لِحَيَاتِى (٢٤)
He will say, ‘Alas, had I sent ahead for my life [in the Hereafter]!’ (24)
فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُعَذِّبُ عَذَابَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ (٢٥)
On that day none shall punish as He punishes, (25)
وَلَا يُوثِقُ وَثَاقَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ (٢٦)
and none shall bind as He binds. (26)
يَـٰٓأَيَّتُهَا ٱلنَّفْسُ ٱلْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ (٢٧)
‘O soul at peace! (27)
ٱرْجِعِىٓ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَّرْضِيَّةً (٢٨)
Return to your Lord, pleased and pleasing! (28)
فَٱدْخُلِى فِى عِبَـٰدِى (٢٩)
Then enter among My servants, (29)
وَٱدْخُلِى جَنَّتِى (٣٠)
and enter My paradise!’ (30)

(Commentary)

The Surah contains a condemnation of attachment to the world (الدنيا), which leads to tyranny (الطغيان) and ingratitude (الكفران), and it threatens its people with the severest of Allah’s punishment in this world and the Hereafter. It clarifies that man, due to his limited vision and poor thinking, perceives that what Allah has granted him of His blessings is a sign of his nobility (كرامته) in the sight of Allah, and that what afflicts him of poverty and lack is a sign of his humiliation (هوانه). Consequently, he becomes tyrannical and spreads corruption in the earth when he possesses, and disbelieves when he lacks. The matter has become confused for him; for the power and wealth that reach him, as well as the poverty and straitened means of subsistence, are but a divine trial (امتحان) and a test (ابتلاء) to manifest what he puts forward from his worldly life for his Hereafter.

The matter is not as man imagines and says; rather, the matter is as he will remember when the Reckoning (الحساب) occurs and the Punishment (العذاب) is present: that what afflicted him of poverty or wealth, or strength or weakness, was a divine trial, and he could have put forward from his today for his tomorrow, but he did not, preferring punishment over reward. None shall attain the felicitous life in the Hereafter except the soul at peace (النفس المطمئنة) with its Lord, submitting to His command—that which is not shaken by the storms of trials, nor does possession make it tyrannical, nor does loss make it ungrateful.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْفَجْرِ وَلَيالٍ عَشْرٍ وَالشَّفْعِ وَالْوَتْرِ وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَسْرِ هَلْ فِي ذلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِذِي حِجْرٍ — By the dawn, and the ten nights, and the even and the odd, and the night when it departs! Is there an oath in that for one possessed of intellect? (89:1-5). The dawn (الفجر) is the morning. The even (الشفع) is the pair; al-Raghib said: "The even (الشفع) is the joining of a thing to its like, and the joined thing is called even." End of quote. The "departing" (سري) of the night is its passing and retreating. Intellect (الحجر) is the reason (العقل). Thus, His saying: وَالْفَجْرِ is an oath by the morning, and the same applies to what is conjoined to it of the nights, the even, the odd, and the night.

Perhaps the apparent meaning of His saying: وَالْفَجْرِ is that it refers to dawn in general. It is also not improbable that it refers to the dawn of the Day of Sacrifice (يوم النحر), which is the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah.

It has been said: It refers to the dawn of Dhu al-Hijjah. It was said: The dawn of al-Muharram, the beginning of the year. It was said: The dawn of Friday. It was said: The dawn of the night of Jam‘ (Muzdalifah). It was said: It refers to the dawn prayer (صلاة الفجر). It was said: The entire day. It was said: The gushing (فجر) of springs from rocks and elsewhere. These are weak views.

His saying: وَلَيالٍ عَشْرٍ — and the ten nights — perhaps refers to the ten nights from the beginning of Dhu al-Hijjah to its tenth, and the use of the indefinite form is for magnification (التفخيم).

It was said: They are the last ten nights of the month of Ramadan. It was said: The first ten nights of it. It was said: The first ten nights of al-Muharram. It was said: It refers to the worship of ten nights, assuming "the dawn" refers to the dawn prayer.

His saying: وَالشَّفْعِ وَالْوَتْرِ — and the even and the odd — is applicable to the Day of Tarwiyah and the Day of ‘Arafah, which is more appropriate if "the dawn" and "the ten nights" are taken to mean the dawn of Dhu al-Hijjah and its first ten nights.

It was said: It refers to the shaf‘ and watr prayers at the end of the night. It was said: Prayer in general, for some are even and some are odd. It was said: The even is the Day of Sacrifice and the odd is the Day of ‘Arafah. It was said: The even is all of creation because He said: وَخَلَقْناكُمْ أَزْواجاً — and created you in pairs (78:8), and the odd is Allah, the Exalted. Regarding these views, there are narrations that will reach you in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

It was said: It refers to even and odd numbers; the oath by them is a reminder of number (العدد), due to the great blessing from Allah, the Glorified, in the precision of measures through it. It was said: The even and the odd are all created things, because things are either even or odd. It was said: The odd is Adam, who was made even by his wife. It was said: The even is the days and nights, and the odd is the day after which there is no night, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). It was said: The even is al-Safa and al-Marwah, and the odd is the Sacred House (البيت الحرام). It was said: The even is the days of ‘Ad and the odd is its nights. It was said: The even is the gates of Paradise, which are eight, and the odd is the gates of Hell, which are seven—among other views. They are many; some have counted them up to thirty-six views, most of which are not free from arbitrariness (تحكم).

His saying: وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَسْرِ — and the night when it departs — meaning it passes. It is like His saying: وَاللَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ — and by the night when it retreats (74:33). Its apparent meaning is that the definite article (اللام) is for the genus (للجنس), so it refers to the end of the night in general. It was said: It refers to the night of al-Muzdalifah, which is the night of the Sacrifice in which the pilgrim travels (يسري) from ‘Arafat to al-Muzdalifah, gathering therein upon the obedience of Allah, then departing from it to Mina. This is as you see—especially based on the view that the "ten nights" are the first ten nights of the month.

His saying: هَلْ فِي ذلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِذِي حِجْرٍ — Is there an oath in that for one possessed of intellect? The demonstrative "that" (ذلِكَ) refers to the preceding oaths. The interrogation is for affirmation (للتقرير). The meaning is that in what we have presented, there is a sufficient oath for one who possesses an intellect (عقل) by which he understands speech and distinguishes truth from falsehood. When Allah, the Glorified, swears by a matter—and He only swears by that which possesses nobility and status—it becomes a truthful, emphasized statement in whose veracity there is no doubt.

The response (جواب) to the aforementioned oaths is omitted (محذوف), indicated by what will be mentioned of the punishment of the people of tyranny and ingratitude in this world and the Hereafter, the reward of the souls at peace, and that His blessing upon those He blesses and His withholding from those He withholds is only a trial and a test.

The omission of the response and the allusion to it by way of metonymy (التكنية) is more eloquent and emphatic in the context of warning and glad tidings.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعادٍ — Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with ‘Ad (89:6). They are the first ‘Ad, the people of Hud (عليه السلام). Their story is repeated in the Holy Quran, and it is indicated that they were in al-Ahqaf. We have already presented what can be gathered from their stories in the Quran in the commentary of Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: إِرَمَ ذاتِ الْعِمادِ الَّتِي لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُها فِي الْبِلادِ — [the people of] Iram, [the city] of columns, the like of which was not created in [any of] the lands? (89:7-8). The column (العماد), the plural of which is ‘amad, is that upon which buildings rely. The apparent meaning of the two verses is that Iram was a populated city of theirs, without peer, possessing high palaces and extended columns. The reports of these people have been cut off since their time and their traces have been erased. There is no way to obtain details of their condition that would satisfy the soul except what the Holy Quran narrates in the summary of their story: that they came after the people of Noah, resided in al-Ahqaf, were of great physical stature, and possessed great strength and might. They had progress and advancement in civilization and culture, possessing populated lands and fertile territories with gardens, palms, crops, and a noble station. The story has been previously discussed.

It was said: Iram refers to the people of ‘Ad—it was originally the name of their forefather, and they were named after him, just as it is said "Quraysh" meaning the Qurayshis, or "Israel" meaning the Children of Israel. The meaning of them being "of columns" (ذات عماد) is that they were people of strength and power.

The meaning would be: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the people of ‘Ad, who are the people of Iram, the possessors of strength and intensity, the like of whom were not created in physical stature, strength, and might in the lands or the regions of the earth? This is not free from being far-fetched according to the literal wording.

Even more far-fetched is what was said: that their being "of columns" means they were people of mobile tents (عمد سيارة) in the spring; when the vegetation flourished, they would return to their dwellings.

Among the legends is the famous story of the Garden of Iram narrated from Wahb ibn Munabbih and Ka‘b al-Ahbar.

His saying, the Exalted: وَثَمُودَ الَّذِينَ جابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوادِ — and Thamud, who hollowed out the rocks in the valley? (89:9). Hollowing (الجوب) is cutting; meaning they cut the rocks of the mountains by carving them into houses. It is in the sense of His saying: وَتَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً — and hew out houses from the mountains (26:149).

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتادِ — and Pharaoh, the impaler [of men]? (89:10). He is the Pharaoh of Moses. He was called "the possessor of stakes" (ذو الأوتاد)—according to some narrations—because when he wanted to punish a man, he would stretch him out on the ground and stake his hands and feet with four stakes into the earth; sometimes he would stretch him on wood and do that to him. This is supported by what Allah narrated of his saying when he threatened the magicians when they believed in Moses: وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ فِي جُذُوعِ النَّخْلِ — and I will surely crucify you on the trunks of palm trees (20:71), for they used to stake the hands and feet of the crucified person onto the wood of the cross.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلادِ فَأَكْثَرُوا فِيهَا الْفَسادَ — who rebelled in the lands, and spread much corruption in them? (89:11-12). This is a description of those mentioned: ‘Ad, Thamud, and Pharaoh. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذابٍ — So your Lord poured on them a lash of punishment (89:13). The pouring (صب) of water is well-known, and the "pouring of the lash of punishment" is a metaphor (كناية) for continuous, successive, and severe torment. The use of the indefinite "punishment" (عذاب) is for magnification.

The meaning is: Your Lord sent down upon each of these tyrants who increased corruption, following their tyranny and excessive corruption, a severe, continuous, and successive punishment that cannot be described.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ — Indeed your Lord is in ambush (المرصاد) — the place from which one lies in wait and observes (89:14). Allah being "in ambush" is a representational metaphor (استعارة تمثيلية) in which His preservation of the deeds of His servants is likened to one who sits in ambush, watching the one intended to be watched, then seizing him when he passes by while he is unaware. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is an observer (رقيب) who watches the deeds of His servants until, when they transgress and increase corruption, He seizes them with the severest punishment.

In the verse is the justification (تعليل) for what preceded regarding the punishment of the tyrants who increased corruption among those of the past. In His saying: رَبَّكَ (your Lord), by adding "Lord" to the second-person pronoun, there is an allusion that the sunnah (سنة) of punishment is applicable to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) nation just as it was applicable to the past nations.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا الْإِنْسانُ إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ — As for man, whenever his Lord tests him, and honors him, and blesses him, he says, ‘My Lord has honored me.’ (89:15). This is branched from what preceded; it contains a detail of the state of man when he is given the blessings of the world or deprived of them. It is as if it were said: Man is under divine observation; his Lord watches him to see if he will act righteously or corruptly. He tries him and tests him in what He gives him of blessing or deprivation. This is the reality of the matter in itself. As for man, when Allah bestows a blessing upon him, he thinks that it is a divine honoring of him to do with it as he pleases, so he becomes tyrannical and increases corruption. And when He withholds and restricts his provision, he thinks it is a divine humiliation, so he disbelieves and despairs.

So His saying: فَأَمَّا الْإِنْسانُ (As for man)—the intended meaning is the species according to its primary nature (الطبع الأولي); thus the definite article (اللام) is for the genus, not for total inclusion (الاستغراق).

His saying: إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ — meaning He tried him and tested him. The operator (العامل) for the adverbial phrase is omitted, its estimation being "it is so when..." etc. It was said: The operator is فَيَقُولُ (he says).

His saying: فَأَكْرَمَهُ وَنَعَّمَهُ — is an explanation of the trial (الابتلاء). The intended "honoring" and "blessing" are the formal ones; or if you wish, say: the honoring and blessing in their inception (حدوثاً), not in their permanence (بقاءً). That is, He, the Exalted, honored him and gave him the blessing so that he might thank Him and worship Him, but he turned it into a vengeance upon himself that entails punishment.

His saying: فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ — meaning: He has placed me in a state of nobility from Him through the blessings He gave me. Or if you wish, say: the power and wealth granted are an honoring and blessing in inception and permanence, so I may do as I wish.

The sentence, namely His saying: فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ, is a narration of what man perceives according to his nature. Man's saying: "My Lord has honored me," which appears to attribute governance (التدبير) to Allah, the Glorified—though the idolaters and those who deny the Creator do not say this—is based on his recognition of Him, the Exalted, according to the primordial nature (الفطرة), even if he rejects it with his tongue. Also, it is to maintain the correspondence with His saying: إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ رَبُّهُ.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّي أَهانَنِ — But when He tests him and tightens his provision, he says, ‘My Lord has humiliated me.’ (89:16). Meaning: but when He tries him and tests him, and thus narrows his provision, he says: My Lord has debased me and made light of me.

From the sum of the two verses, it appears: First, since He repeated the trial (الابتلاء) and affirmed it in both cases of blessing and withholding, that the giving of blessings and the withholding of them are both part of the divine trial and test, as He said: وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً — and We test you with good and ill by way of trial (21:35), not as man perceives it.

Second, that the giving of blessings, insofar as it is grace and mercy, is an honoring if man does not change them into vengeance upon himself.

Third, that the two verses together signify that man sees his happiness in life as being blessed in the world with Allah's favors, which is "honoring" to him, and deprivation from it as "misery" to him. In reality, honoring lies in proximity to Him, the Exalted, through faith (الإيمان) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), whether in wealth or poverty, and in any possession or loss; for that is only a trial and a test.

There are other views regarding the meaning of the two verses which we have omitted due to their lack of benefit.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا بَلْ لا تُكْرِمُونَ الْيَتِيمَ وَلا تَحَاضُّونَ عَلى طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ — No indeed! Rather you do not honor the orphan, and do not urge one another to feed the needy (89:17-18). This is a rebuff (ردع) of their saying that honoring lies in wealth and luxury, and that in poverty and loss there is humiliation and debasement. The meaning is: It is not as you say; rather, His giving of blessing and His withholding of it are all a trial and a test by which the state of man is tested in terms of his servitude (عبودية).

In His saying: بَلْ لا تُكْرِمُونَ الْيَتِيمَ etc., there is a transition (إضراب) that emphasizes the rebuff by mentioning some of the luxury that is absolutely incompatible with true honoring, such as their lack of honoring the orphan by consuming his inheritance (تراث) and depriving him of it, and the lack of urging one another to feed the needy out of love for wealth. The human primordial nature (الفطرة) does not doubt that there is no honoring in wealth of this nature.

In the transition, in addition to the original rebuff, there is a reprimand (تقريع). To intensify this reprimand, a shift (التفات) occurred from the third person to the second person.

So His saying: بَلْ لا تُكْرِمُونَ الْيَتِيمَ — the lack of honoring him is depriving him of his father's inheritance—as they used to deprive young children of inheritance—and leaving him empty-handed no matter how much hardship he reached, as supported by the following verse: وَتأْكُلُونَ التُّراثَ etc.

His saying: وَلا تَحَاضُّونَ عَلى طَعامِ الْمِسْكِينِ — its origin is la tatahaddun, which is the urging of one another to give charity to the destitute needy. Its source is the love of wealth, as in the coming verse: وَتُحِبُّونَ الْمالَ etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَأْكُلُونَ التُّراثَ أَكْلاً لَمًّا — and you devour the inheritance greedily (89:19). Greed (اللم) is a person's eating of his own share and that of others, and his eating of whatever he finds without distinguishing the wholesome from the foul. The verse is an explanation of their lack of honoring the orphan, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتُحِبُّونَ الْمالَ حُبًّا جَمًّا — and you love wealth with much fondness (89:20). Much (الجم) is that which is abundant and great. The verse explains their lack of urging one another to feed the needy, as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا إِذا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا — No indeed! When the earth is crushed to fine dust (89:21). Crushing (الدك) is severe pounding. The intended time is the presence of the Day of Resurrection.

This is a second rebuff of what man says in the states of wealth and poverty. His saying: إِذا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ etc., is in the position of justifying the rebuff. The sum of the meaning is: It is not as man says, for he will remember when the Resurrection is established that the worldly life and what is in it of wealth, poverty, and the like were not intended for their own sake, but were a trial and a test from Allah, the Exalted, by which He distinguishes the felicitous from the wretched, and in which man prepares what he will live by in the Hereafter. The matter was confused for him, so he thought of them as an honoring intended for their own sake; thus he occupied himself with them and did not put forward anything for his life in the Hereafter. At that time, he will wish and say: "O, would that I had put forward for my life!" But wishing will not avert any of the punishment from him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا — and your Lord comes with the angels in ranks (89:22). The attribution of "coming" (المجيء) to Him, the Exalted, is among the ambiguous (المتشابه) matters which are governed by His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — Nothing is like Him (42:11), and what has been mentioned in the verses of the Resurrection regarding the characteristics of that day, such as the severing of causes, the lifting of veils from them, and the manifestation that Allah is the Manifest Truth.

To this returns what has been mentioned in the narrations: that the intended meaning of His coming is the coming of His command. He, the Exalted, said: وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ — and the command that day will belong to Allah (82:19). This view is somewhat supported by His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ اللهُ فِي ظُلَلٍ مِنَ الْغَمامِ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ وَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ — Do they await anything but that Allah should come to them in the shadows of the clouds, with the angels, and the matter be decided? (2:210), when joined with His saying: هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ — Do they await anything but that the angels should come to them, or your Lord’s edict should come? (16:33). Accordingly, there is an omitted possessed noun (مضاف محذوف), and the estimation is "the command of your Lord came," or the attribution of coming to Him, the Exalted, is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي).

The discussion regarding the attribution of coming to the angels and their being in ranks is as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجِيءَ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِجَهَنَّمَ — and hell is brought near on that day (89:23) to the end of the verse. It is not improbable that the "bringing" of Hell means its manifestation to them, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَبُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِمَنْ يرى — and hell is made manifest for him who sees (79:36), and His saying: وَبُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِلْغاوِينَ — and hell is made manifest for the perverse (26:91), and His saying: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today.’ (50:22).

His saying: يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ — on that day man will take admonition — meaning he will remember with the clearest remembrance that what he was given in the worldly life of good or evil was from Allah's trial and test, and that he was negligent in his affair. This is what the context signifies.

His saying: وَأَنَّى لَهُ الذِّكْرى — but how will the admonition avail him? — meaning: from where will the remembrance come to him? This is a metonymy for his lack of benefit from it, for remembrance only benefits when it is possible for him to rectify what he neglected through repentance (توبة) and righteous deeds (عمل صالح), and today is the Day of Recompense, not the day of return and action.

His saying, the Exalted: يَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي قَدَّمْتُ لِحَياتِي — He will say, ‘Alas, I wish I had sent forth [some good deeds] for my life!’ (89:24). Meaning: for this life of mine, which is the life of the Hereafter. Or the intended meaning is the true life, which is the life of the Hereafter, as He, the Exalted, pointed out in His saying: وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحيَوانُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known. (29:64).

The meaning of "sending forth for life" is putting forward righteous deeds for the life of the Hereafter. What is in the verse is a wish that man wishes when he remembers on the Day of Resurrection and witnesses that it does not benefit him.

His saying, the Exalted: فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لا يُعَذِّبُ عَذابَهُ أَحَدٌ وَلا يُوثِقُ وَثاقَهُ أَحَدٌ — On that day none shall punish with a punishment like His, and none shall bind with a binding like His. (89:25-26). The two pronouns in "His punishment" (عذابه) and "His binding" (وثاقه) refer to Allah, the Exalted. The meaning is: on that day, none of the creation shall punish with the punishment of Allah, and none of the creation shall bind with the binding of Allah. That is, His punishment and binding on that day are above the punishment and binding of the creation—an intensification of the threat.

It has been recited as لا يُعَذَّبُ with a fathah on the dhal and وَلا يُوثَقُ with a fathah on the tha’, in the passive voice. The two pronouns in "his punishment" and "his binding" according to this refer to man. The meaning is: none shall be punished on that day like the punishment of [this] man, and none shall be bound on that day like his binding.

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ — O soul at peace! (89:27). What the context of the contrast gives—between this soul with the descriptions mentioned for it and the good return designated for it, and the man mentioned before with the description of attachment to the world, tyranny, corruption, and ingratitude, and the evil destiny he was threatened with—is that the soul at peace (النفس المطمئنة) is that which finds rest in its Lord and is pleased with what He is pleased with. It sees itself as a servant (عبد) who possesses nothing for itself of good or evil, benefit or harm. It sees the world as a place of passage, and what it encounters therein of wealth or poverty, or any benefit or harm, as a divine trial and test. Thus, the succession of blessings upon it does not lead it to tyranny, excessive corruption, haughtiness, and arrogance; nor does poverty and loss cause it to fall into disbelief and the abandonment of gratitude. Rather, it is in a stable state of servitude (العبودية), not deviating from its straight path (صراطه المستقيم) by excess or negligence.

His saying, the Exalted: ارْجِعِي إِلى رَبِّكِ راضِيَةً مَرْضِيَّةً — Return to your Lord, pleased and pleasing! (89:28). A call whose time is the entirety of the Day of Resurrection, from the moment of its being brought to life until its settling in Paradise—rather, from the time of the descent of death until the entry into the Paradise of Eternity. It is not a call occurring after the Reckoning, as some have mentioned.

Its description as "pleased" (راضية) is because its peace in its Lord necessitates its pleasure with what He has decreed and ordained existentially (تكونياً) or judged legislatively (تشريعاً). Thus, no occurrence makes it resentful, and no disobedience makes it deviate. When the servant is pleased with his Lord, the Lord is pleased with him, for nothing displeases Him, the Exalted, except the servant's departure from the garb of servitude. So when he adheres to the path of servitude, that necessitates the pleasure of his Lord. Therefore, He followed His saying: راضِيَةً (pleased) with His saying: مَرْضِيَّةً (pleasing).

His saying, the Exalted: فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبادِي وَادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي — Enter among My servants! And enter My paradise! (89:29-30). A branching from His saying: ارْجِعِي إِلى رَبِّكِ. In it is an indication that the possessor of the soul at peace is among the group of the servants of Allah, having attained the station of servitude (مقام العبودية).

That is because when it found peace in its Lord, it cut itself off from the claim of independence and was pleased with what is the Truth from its Lord. It saw its essence, attributes, and actions as the absolute property of its Lord. It did not desire in what He decreed and ordained, nor in what He commanded and forbade, except what its Lord desired. This is the manifestation of complete servitude (العبودية التامة) in the servant. Thus, in His saying: فَادْخُلِي فِي عِبادِي, there is a confirmation of the station of its servitude.

In His saying: وَادْخُلِي جَنَّتِي, there is a designation of its resting place. In the addition of "Paradise" to the first-person pronoun, there is a special honoring (تشريف). There is no instance in His speech, the Exalted and Sanctified, of adding "Paradise" to Himself except in this verse.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالشَّفْعِ وَالْوَتْرِ, it was said: The even (الشفع) is the creation because He said: وَخَلَقْناكُمْ أَزْواجاً — and created you in pairs, and the odd (الوتر) is Allah, the Exalted. This is from ‘Atiyyah al-‘Awfi, Abu Salih, Ibn ‘Abbas, and Mujahid, and it is a narration of Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It was said: The even and the odd are the prayer, some of which are even and some are odd; this is a narration from Ibn Husayn from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It was said: The even is the Day of Sacrifice and the odd is the Day of ‘Arafah; from Ibn ‘Abbas, ‘Ikrimah, and al-Dahhak, and it is a narration of Jabir from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The reason for it is that the Day of Sacrifice is made even by the day of departure after it, while the Day of ‘Arafah is singular in the standing (بالموقف). It was said: The even is the Day of Tarwiyah and the odd is the Day of ‘Arafah; this was narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام).

I say: The three aforementioned narrations are narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through Sunni paths. It is possible to reconcile them by saying that the intended meaning is even and odd in general, and the narrations are by way of indicating some of the instances (المصاديق).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: وَلَيالٍ عَشْرٍ — he said: The ten of Dhu al-Hijjah. وَالشَّفْعِ وَالْوَتْرِ — he said: The even is two units (ركعتان) and the odd is one unit. In a tradition: The even is al-Hasan and al-Husayn, and the odd is the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام). وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَسْرِ — he said: It is the night of Jam‘.

In it, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: لِذِي حِجْرٍ — he says: For one possessed of intellect (عقل).

In al-‘Ilal, with its chain to Aban al-Ahmar, he said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتادِ — for what reason was he called "the possessor of stakes"? He said: "Because when he punished a man, he would stretch him on the ground on his face and extend his hands and feet, then stake them with four stakes into the ground. Sometimes he would stretch him on a flat piece of wood, then stake his feet and hands with four stakes, then leave him in that state until he died. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, named him Pharaoh, the possessor of stakes."

In al-Majma‘: regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ, it was narrated from ‘Ali (عليه السلام) that he said: "Its meaning is that your Lord is capable of recompensing the people of disobedience with their recompense."

I say: The basis of the narration is taking the sentence as a representational metaphor.

In it, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said: "The ambush (المرصاد) is a bridge on the Path (الصراط) which no servant shall cross who owes a grievance (بمظلمة) to another servant."

From al-Ghawali, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a tradition regarding the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: وَذَا النُّونِ إِذْ ذَهَبَ مُغاضِباً فَظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ نَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ — And [remember] the Man of the Fish, when he left in a rage, thinking We would not stint him (21:87): "He only 'thought' in the sense that he was certain that Allah, the Exalted, would not tighten (لن يضيق) his provision. Do you not hear the saying of Allah, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا إِذا مَا ابْتَلاهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ — meaning He tightened it upon him."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: كَلَّا إِذا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا, he said: "It is the earthquake (الزلزلة)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from ‘Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Do you know what the commentary of this verse is: كَلَّا إِذا دُكَّتِ الْأَرْضُ — until His saying — وَجِيءَ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِجَهَنَّمَ? He said: When it is the Day of Resurrection, Hell shall be led by seventy thousand reins, in the hand of seventy thousand angels. It shall bolt with such a bolting that, were it not that Allah restrained it, it would have burned the heavens and the earth."

I say: It is also narrated from Abu Sa‘id and Ibn Mas‘ud, and through Shi‘i paths in the Amali of the Shaykh, with his chain to Dawud ibn Sulayman from al-Rida from his fathers from ‘Ali (عليه السلام) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In al-‘Uyun, in the chapter on what has come from al-Rida regarding the reports of monotheism (التوحيد), with his chain to ‘Ali ibn Faddal from his father, he said: I asked al-Rida (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَجاءَ رَبُّكَ وَالْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا. He said: "Indeed Allah, the Glorified, is not described by coming and going; He is exalted above movement (الانتقال). It only means by that: 'And the command of your Lord came'."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Sudayr al-Sayrafi, he said: I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام): "May I be your ransom, O son of the Messenger of Allah! Is the believer coerced into the seizing of his soul?" He said: "No, by Allah! When the Angel of Death comes to him to seize his soul, he becomes distressed at that. So the Angel of Death says: 'O friend of Allah, do not be distressed! For by the One who sent Muhammad, I am more dutiful to you and more compassionate toward you than a merciful father were he present with you. Open your eyes and look!'"

He said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), the Commander of the Faithful, Fatimah, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and the Imams from their progeny (عليه السلام) are personified for him. It is said to him: 'This is the Messenger of Allah, the Commander of the Faithful, Fatimah, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and the Imams (عليه السلام); they are your companions'."

He said: "So he opens his eyes and looks. Then a caller calls his soul from the direction of the Lord of Might, saying: 'O soul at peace with Muhammad and his Household! Return to your Lord, pleased with the Mastership (بالولاية) and pleasing with the reward. Enter among My servants—meaning Muhammad and his Household—and enter My Paradise!' There is nothing more beloved to him than the drawing out of his soul and joining the caller."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated this meaning in his Tafsir, as did al-Barqi in al-Mahasin.
